purpose: Safely restart a specific SONiC Docker container with impact assessment
prerequisites: Identify the exact container name: docker ps --format '{{.Names}}'
Confirm the database container is healthy before restarting any other container
If restarting swss or syncd, plan for a 60-120s recovery window and ASIC reprogram
If restarting bgp container, notify peers/NOC of expected session drops
Backup running config before restarting any critical container: sudo sonic-cfggen -d --print-data > /tmp/pre_restart_config.json
Confirm systemd service name matches container: systemctl list-units | grep docker
warnings: NEVER restart the database container in production — it holds CONFIG_DB, APPL_DB, and STATE_DB in Redis; a crash or failed restart loses all runtime state and requires a full config reload.
Restarting swss triggers a full ASIC reprogram via orchagent/syncd reconciliation — expect 30-120s of forwarding disruption on the impacted ASIC.
Restarting syncd alone is rarely safe; it disconnects from the ASIC SAI layer and leaves orchagent without a programming backend until syncd reconnects.
Restarting teamd causes LACP PDU transmission to stop; remote switches will age out the LAG after actor-timeout (default 90s), causing link bundle failure.
Container restart via 'docker restart' does NOT reinitialize systemd dependencies — always use 'sudo systemctl restart <container>' to respect service ordering.
If a container OOM-killed repeatedly, restarting it will reproduce the OOM — investigate memory pressure first with 'docker stats' and 'free -m'.

container_impact_reference.impact: Loss of all Redis databases; full config reload required
ASIC SAI disconnected; full ASIC reprogram on restart
orchagent exits; APPL_DB programming stops; ASIC reprogram on restart
All BGP/OSPF/BFD sessions drop; routes withdraw; convergence on restart
LACP PDUs stop; LAG may go down after actor-timeout
container_impact_reference.forwarding_impact: LAG bundle failure if timeout exceeded

container_impact_reference.impact: LLDP neighbor discovery paused; no forwarding impact

steps.action: Identify the target container and confirm it is the one causing issues

verification.look_for: Container status (running/exited/restarting), exit code (0=clean, non-zero=crash), and start time
verification.confirms_if: Container is identified and its current state is understood before proceeding

steps.notes: Replace <container> with the actual container name (e.g. swss, bgp, syncd). If the container is in a restart loop, check: docker inspect <container> | grep -A5 RestartPolicy; journalctl -u docker@<container> --no-pager -n 50

steps.action: Assess container criticality and determine forwarding impact using the impact reference table

verification.command: sonic-db-cli CONFIG_DB keys 'DEVICE_METADATA|localhost' | xargs -I{} sonic-db-cli CONFIG_DB HGETALL {}
verification.look_for: Platform, hwsku, and deployment context to gauge blast radius
verification.confirms_if: You understand whether this is a spine/leaf/ToR, traffic volume, and whether a maintenance window is needed

steps.warning: If the target container is database or syncd, STOP and treat this as a high-risk change requiring a maintenance window. If container is swss, confirm with NOC before proceeding.
steps.notes: Check container CPU/memory consumption — if a container is pegging CPU it may be in a tight loop and restart will reproduce the same behavior after a brief recovery.

steps.action: Capture pre-restart state snapshot for post-restart comparison
steps.command: show bgp summary; show interfaces status; show ip route summary; show vlan brief; show mac address-table count

verification.command: show bgp summary | grep -cE 'Estab|Idle|Active'; show ip route summary | grep 'Total'
verification.look_for: BGP peer count and state, total route count, interface up/down counts
verification.confirms_if: Baseline captured and saved for comparison after restart

steps.notes: Save output to a timestamped file: show bgp summary > /tmp/pre_restart_bgp_$(date +%H%M%S).txt. For teamd containers also run: show teamd status. For pmon: show interface transceiver eeprom | head -30.

steps.action: Check for in-progress reconciliation or warm restart state that would be disrupted

verification.look_for: Any component showing state != 'reconciled' or still in 'replayed/initialized' — restarting now would interrupt reconciliation
verification.confirms_if: No warm restart in progress; safe to proceed with container restart

steps.warning: If a warm restart reconciliation is in progress, wait for it to complete (state=reconciled) before restarting any container. Interrupting reconciliation can leave ASIC state inconsistent.
steps.notes: Also check for pending config loads: redis-cli -n 4 HGETALL 'CONFIG_MIGRATION|status'. If a migration is running, abort the restart and investigate.

steps.action: Restart the container using systemctl to respect service dependencies

verification.look_for: systemctl reports 'active (running)'; docker ps shows container Up with recent timestamp
verification.confirms_if: Container process started and systemd considers it active

daemon_impact.impact: Exits and restarts when swss container restarts; re-reads APPL_DB and reprograms ASIC
Disconnects SAI adapter on restart; ASIC programming halted until reconnection
All peering sessions drop on bgp container restart; BGP hold timer expires on peers
LAG configuration pushed from CONFIG_DB on swss or teamd container restart
LLDP neighbor table cleared on lldp container restart; repopulates after tx-interval
Transceiver DOM readings unavailable during pmon container restart

steps.warning: Use 'sudo systemctl restart <container>' NOT 'docker restart <container>'. Docker restart bypasses systemd dependency ordering and can leave orchagent/syncd in a split-brain state.
steps.notes: Container names map 1:1 to systemd service names in SONiC 202511. If systemctl restart fails with 'Unit not found', check: systemctl list-units --type=service | grep <container>. Some platforms use docker@<container>.service as the unit name.

steps.action: Monitor container startup and health during the recovery window

verification.command: docker ps --filter name=<container> --format '{{.Status}}'; journalctl -u <container> --no-pager -n 30 --since '2 minutes ago'
verification.look_for: Container shows 'Up X seconds' with no 'Restarting' or 'Exited' state; logs show initialization complete messages
verification.confirms_if: Container started cleanly without crash loop and initialization sequence completed

steps.notes: Expected startup times by container: database ~5s, swss ~30-90s (ASIC reprogram), syncd ~30-60s, bgp ~10-20s, teamd ~10s, lldp ~5s, pmon ~5s. If swss is taking >120s, check orchagent reconciliation: docker exec swss supervisorctl status

steps.action: Post-restart verification: compare state against pre-restart snapshot
steps.command: show bgp summary; show interfaces status; show ip route summary

verification.command: show bgp summary | grep -cE 'Estab'; show ip route summary | grep 'Total'; show interfaces status | grep -c ' up '
verification.look_for: BGP established peer count matches pre-restart baseline; route count matches; interface up count unchanged
verification.confirms_if: Protocol state fully restored to pre-restart levels

steps.notes: If BGP session count is lower than baseline, check: show bgp neighbors <peer> | grep 'BGP state'. If routes are missing: show ip route | grep -c 'B>'. For swss restarts, also verify: redis-cli -n 0 hlen NEIGH_TABLE:Ethernet0 to confirm ARP/NDP entries repopulated.

steps.command: ping -c 5 <gateway-ip>; show interfaces counters rate | head -20

verification.command: show interfaces counters rate | awk '$5 > 0 || $6 > 0' | head -20
verification.look_for: Interfaces with expected traffic show non-zero TX/RX packet rates; ping to gateway succeeds
verification.confirms_if: Data plane is forwarding traffic normally after container restart

steps.notes: For deeper forwarding verification after swss/syncd restart: sudo vtysh -c 'show ip fib' to compare FIB entries vs RIB; use 'show arp' and 'show ndp' to verify ARP/NDP table repopulated in ASIC via orchagent. If counters show RX but no TX, suspect ASIC programming incomplete — check orchagent logs: docker exec swss cat /var/log/swss/orchagent.log | tail -50

rollback.action: If systemctl restart fails or container enters a crash loop, attempt a forced stop and manual start
rollback.command: sudo systemctl stop <container>; sleep 5; sudo systemctl start <container>; systemctl status <container>
rollback.notes: If the container keeps crashing (ExitCode != 0), do NOT keep restarting it. Investigate root cause first: journalctl -u <container> -n 100 --no-pager; docker logs <container> 2>&1 | tail -50. Common causes: missing config files, Redis connection failure, SAI adapter init failure. If Redis is down, the database container must be restored first before any other container can start cleanly.

rollback.action: If post-restart state does not match baseline (missing routes, BGP sessions down, interfaces flapped), reload config from backup
rollback.notes: Config load pushes CONFIG_DB entries back to Redis; orchagent will reconcile APPL_DB and reprogram the ASIC. After config load, run: sudo config reload -y if a full service restart is needed. Monitor with: show bgp summary; show interfaces status. If the issue is ARP/NDP table not repopulating, try: docker exec swss supervisorctl restart neighsyncd

rollback.action: Emergency: if swss or syncd cannot recover and forwarding is completely black-holed, perform a fast-reboot to restore a clean state
rollback.notes: Fast-reboot performs a warm-restart-assisted reboot with sub-30s forwarding disruption on most platforms. Use this only if individual container restart attempts have failed repeatedly and the ASIC is in an inconsistent state. Capture logs before rebooting: sudo tar czf /tmp/sonic_diag_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).tar.gz /var/log/swss /var/log/syslog
